name: Feather of Maat
description: The Feather of Maat is a central symbol in ancient Egyptian mythology, representing truth, justice, morality, and cosmic balance. It is most famously associated with the Weighing of the Heart ceremony in the afterlife, where the deceased's heart was placed on one side of a scale and the Feather of Maat on the other. If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul was deemed worthy and allowed to pass into the afterlife, ruled by Osiris. If the heart was heavier, burdened by sin and wrongdoing, it would be devoured by the monstrous Ammit, resulting in the soul's eternal destruction. The feather itself is believed to have originated from the headdress of the goddess Maat, who personified these virtues. Its presence in the underworld ritual underscored the importance of living a life guided by truth and righteousness, as only those who had upheld Maat's principles could hope to achieve eternal life. The feather's delicate nature symbolizes the fragility of balance and the ease with which it can be disrupted by injustice and moral failings. Therefore, it served as a constant reminder to the ancient Egyptians to strive for harmony and integrity in their daily lives. The Feather of Maat is not merely an object; it is a potent emblem of the cosmic order and the ethical responsibilities of humanity.
subtitle: Truth, Justice, and Cosmic Balance
origin: From the headdress of the goddess Maat

keyMyths.title: Weighing of the Heart
keyMyths.description: The deceased's heart is weighed against the Feather of Maat to determine their worthiness to enter the afterlife. If the heart is lighter, the soul is granted eternal life.

keyMyths.title: Maat's Role
keyMyths.description: Maat, the goddess of truth and justice, embodies the principles represented by the feather. She maintains cosmic order and ensures fairness in the universe.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: Translation and analysis of the ancient Egyptian funerary text.
sources.text: Translation and analysis of the ancient Egyptian funerary text.

sources.author: Carolyn Graves-Brown
sources.work: Dancing for Hathor: Women in Ancient Egypt
